nodes.name: Famous Mathematicians
nodes.description: Key mathematicians and their groundbreaking contributions

items.prompt: What is the most prestigious international award in mathematics, often called the 'Nobel Prize of math,' awarded every four years to up to four mathematicians under the age of 40?
items.explanation:
Awarded every four years at the International Congress of Mathematicians (ICM) since 1936
Named after Canadian mathematician John Charles Fields; carries a cash prize of just 15,000 Canadian dollars
The age limit of 40 is strict — Andrew Wiles was ineligible despite proving Fermat's Last Theorem
Frequently called the 'Nobel Prize of mathematics,' though mathematicians debate the comparison
64 people have won through 2022; the next ceremony is 2026
The medal features Archimedes on the front and a Latin inscription meaning 'to transcend one's spirit and take hold of the world'

items.prompt: What annual prize in mathematics, established by Norway in 2003 and named after a 19th-century Norwegian who died at 26, is awarded for lifetime achievement with no age restriction?
items.explanation:
Awarded annually by the Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters since 2003
Named after Niels Henrik Abel (1802–1829), who proved the impossibility of solving the general quintic equation
Worth approximately 7.5 million Norwegian kroner (~$700,000)
Created partly to fill the gap left by the absence of a Nobel Prize in mathematics
Jean-Pierre Serre was the inaugural laureate in 2003
Masaki Kashiwara won in 2025, becoming the first Japanese recipient

items.prompt: What academic discipline is famously absent from the Nobel Prize categories, a gap that led to the creation of the Fields Medal and Abel Prize as its highest honors?
items.explanation:
Alfred Nobel's will (1895) established prizes in Physics, Chemistry, Medicine, Literature, and Peace; Economics was added in 1968
The popular legend that Nobel excluded math because a mathematician stole his wife is almost certainly false — Nobel never married
More likely Nobel simply didn't consider pure mathematics a 'practical' benefit to humanity, which his prizes were meant to reward
Some mathematicians have won the Nobel in other fields: John Nash (Economics, 1994), Bertrand Russell (Literature, 1950)
The Fields Medal (1936) and Abel Prize (2003) were both created partly to address this gap
The Wolf Prize in Mathematics (1978) and Breakthrough Prize in Mathematics (2013) also serve as major honors

items.prompt: Which reclusive Russian mathematician proved the Poincaré conjecture in 2003, then refused both the Fields Medal in 2006 and the $1 million Clay Millennium Prize in 2010?
items.explanation:
Born 1966 in Leningrad (now St. Petersburg), Russia
Proved the Poincaré conjecture by proving Thurston's geometrization conjecture using Ricci flow with surgery
Posted his proof on arXiv in three papers (2002–2003) rather than submitting to a peer-reviewed journal
Declined the Fields Medal in 2006: 'I'm not interested in money or fame. I don't want to be on display like an animal in a zoo.'
Declined the $1 million Millennium Prize in 2010 — the only person to refuse either award
Lives with his mother in St. Petersburg; reportedly quit mathematics entirely after the proof
His proof of the Poincaré conjecture remains the only Millennium Prize Problem solved to date

items.prompt: Which Iranian mathematician became the first woman to win the Fields Medal in 2014 for her work on the dynamics and geometry of Riemann surfaces, before dying of breast cancer at age 40?
items.explanation:
Born 1977 in Tehran, Iran; died 2017 at Stanford, California, of breast cancer at age 40
First and (as of 2025) only woman to win the Fields Medal, awarded in 2014
Worked on Riemann surfaces, hyperbolic geometry, and Teichmüller theory at Stanford University
Won gold medals at the International Mathematical Olympiad in 1994 and 1995, achieving a perfect score in 1995
Known for working on large sheets of paper spread on the floor, which her daughter called 'painting'
Iran's president Hassan Rouhani broke protocol to publish her photo without a hijab after her death
May 12 (her birthday) is now celebrated as International Women in Mathematics Day

items.prompt: Which Australian-American mathematician, who won a gold medal at the International Mathematical Olympiad at age 13 and received the Fields Medal in 2006, is often called the 'Mozart of Math'?
items.explanation:
Born 1975 in Adelaide, Australia, to Chinese immigrant parents
Youngest person to win an IMO gold medal — at age 13 in 1988; also holds records for youngest bronze (age 10) and silver (age 11)
Awarded the Fields Medal in 2006 for contributions to partial differential equations, combinatorics, harmonic analysis, and number theory
Professor at UCLA since age 24; has published over 350 research papers across dozens of fields
Often called the 'Mozart of Math' for the breadth and fluency of his work
Also won the Breakthrough Prize in Mathematics (2014, $3 million) and the MacArthur Fellowship (2006)
Co-proved the Green–Tao theorem: the primes contain arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions

items.prompt: Which German-born French mathematician, who won the Fields Medal in 1966 for revolutionizing algebraic geometry, later became a recluse and lived in seclusion in a Pyrenean village until his death in 2014?
items.explanation:
Born 1928 in Berlin; died 2014 in Lasserre, Ariège, France, at age 86
Revolutionized algebraic geometry with schemes, topoi, and étale cohomology; awarded the Fields Medal in 1966
Stateless for much of his life — his father was a Russian-Jewish anarchist murdered at Auschwitz
Abruptly left the mathematical establishment in 1970 after discovering his institute received military funding
Moved to the village of Lasserre in the Pyrenees in 1991 and lived as a recluse for 23 years
Wrote a 929-page autobiographical manuscript, *Récoltes et Semailles* (Harvests and Sowings), unpublished in his lifetime
Widely considered the most influential mathematician of the second half of the 20th century

items.prompt: Which French mathematician became the youngest-ever Fields Medal winner at age 27 in 1954 and later won the inaugural Abel Prize in 2003, making him one of the most decorated mathematicians alive?
items.explanation:
Born 1926 in Bâges, France
Youngest person ever to win the Fields Medal — age 27 in 1954, for work on homotopy theory and algebraic topology
First recipient of the Abel Prize in 2003
Also won the Wolf Prize (2000), Balzan Prize (1985), and Steele Prize (1995) — virtually every major math award
Made foundational contributions to algebraic geometry, algebraic number theory, and group theory
Known for exceptionally clear mathematical writing; his books are considered models of exposition
Still active in his late 90s; was reportedly still making Wikipedia edits and posting arXiv preprints into 2024
items.cardFront: Youngest Fields Medal (27), first Abel Prize

links.text: Youngest Fields Medal, first Abel Prize

items.prompt: Which French mathematician, instantly recognizable for his trademark spider brooches and lavallière ties, won the Fields Medal in 2010 and later became a member of the French National Assembly?
items.explanation:
Born 1973 in Brive-la-Gaillarde, France
Awarded the Fields Medal in 2010 for work on optimal transport theory and Landau damping
Known for his flamboyant style: always wears spider brooches (he owns dozens), lavallière neckties, and three-piece suits
Elected to the French National Assembly in 2017 as a member of Macron's La République En Marche! party
Director of the Institut Henri Poincaré in Paris (2009–2017)
Wrote the popular math book *Birth of a Theorem* (2012), describing the creative process of mathematical research
Often called the 'Lady Gaga of mathematics' for his public visibility and eccentric appearance

items.prompt: Which Korean-American mathematician, who dropped out of college and only discovered mathematics in his mid-twenties through a chance encounter with a Fields Medalist's lectures, himself won the Fields Medal in 2022?
items.explanation:
Born 1983 in Stanford, California; raised in South Korea
Originally aspired to be a science journalist; discovered mathematics only after attending Heisuke Hironaka's lectures at Seoul National University
Awarded the Fields Medal in 2022 for bringing ideas from Hodge theory to combinatorics
Had a rocky academic start — dropped out of Seoul National University, poor early grades, rejected by most PhD programs
Admitted to the University of Michigan's PhD program largely on Hironaka's recommendation alone
Now a professor at Princeton University
His unconventional path is often cited as proof that mathematical talent can emerge at any age

items.prompt: Which Ukrainian mathematician won the Fields Medal in 2022 for proving that the E₈ lattice provides the densest packing of identical spheres in eight dimensions, becoming only the second woman to win the prize?
items.explanation:
Born 1984 in Kyiv, Ukraine
Second woman to win the Fields Medal, after Maryam Mirzakhani (2014)
Proved in 2016 that the E₈ lattice is the densest sphere packing in 8 dimensions — a problem open for decades
Extended the result to 24 dimensions (Leech lattice) within a week, with collaborators
The proof was remarkably short and elegant, described by colleagues as 'magical'
Professor at the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL) in Switzerland
Her Fields Medal in 2022 came during Russia's invasion of Ukraine, adding symbolic weight to the award
items.cardFront: E₈ sphere packing, second woman Fields Medalist

links.text: E₈ sphere packing, second woman Fields Medalist

items.prompt: Which prolific Hungarian mathematician, who published over 1,500 papers with more than 500 collaborators and lived as an itinerant scholar with no permanent home, inspired the concept of a collaborative-distance 'number' named after him?
items.explanation:
Born 1913 in Budapest; died 1996 in Warsaw during a math conference at age 83
Published approximately 1,525 papers — more than any mathematician except Euler
The 'Erdős number' measures collaborative distance: co-authors have Erdős number 1, their co-authors have 2, etc.
Owned almost nothing; lived out of a suitcase, traveling between collaborators' homes and conferences
Famous for calling children 'epsilons' (small quantities), saying God keeps the best proofs in 'The Book,' and offering cash prizes for solving problems
Consumed prodigious amounts of coffee and amphetamines; once won a bet by abstaining from amphetamines for a month, then said 'You've set mathematics back by a month'
Subject of the documentary *N Is a Number* (1993) and the book *The Man Who Loved Only Numbers* (1998)

items.prompt: What is the only one of the seven Clay Millennium Prize Problems to have been solved, proved by a Russian mathematician in 2003, concerning whether every simply connected closed 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere?
items.explanation:
Posed by Henri Poincaré in 1904: is every simply connected, closed 3-dimensional manifold a 3-sphere?
Proved by Grigori Perelman in 2002–2003 using Richard Hamilton's Ricci flow technique
The only Millennium Prize Problem solved to date — six remain open as of 2026
Perelman declined the $1 million prize, saying 'I know how to control the universe. Why would I run to collect a million dollars?'
Higher-dimensional versions were proved earlier: dimension ≥5 by Stephen Smale (1961), dimension 4 by Michael Freedman (1982)
The conjecture in 3 dimensions was the hardest case and the last to fall
Poincaré himself only stated it as a question, not a conjecture — 'Is it possible that...'

items.prompt: What famous list of 23 unsolved problems, presented by a German mathematician at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris in 1900, set the agenda for much of 20th-century mathematics?
items.explanation:
Presented by David Hilbert at the ICM in Paris on August 8, 1900
Of the 23 problems, about 10 have been fully solved, several partially solved, and some remain open
Problem 1 (continuum hypothesis) was shown to be independent of standard axioms by Gödel (1940) and Cohen (1963)
Problem 2 (consistency of arithmetic) was effectively answered 'no' by Gödel's incompleteness theorems (1931)
Problem 10 (Diophantine equations algorithm) was proved unsolvable by Yuri Matiyasevich in 1970
The Riemann hypothesis (Problem 8) remains unsolved and is also a Millennium Prize Problem
Hilbert's address is considered the most influential speech in the history of mathematics

items.prompt: What annual international mathematics competition for pre-college students, first held in Romania in 1959 with seven participating countries, is the oldest and most prestigious of the international science olympiads?
items.explanation:
First held in 1959 in Bucharest, Romania, with seven Eastern Bloc countries; now draws 100+ countries
Each country sends a team of up to six students; contestants must be under 20 and not enrolled in university
The competition consists of six problems over two days (4.5 hours per day), each worth 7 points (max 42)
Gold, silver, and bronze medals awarded to roughly the top half; a perfect score is exceptionally rare
Terence Tao won gold at age 13 (1988) — the youngest gold medalist in history
Maryam Mirzakhani scored a perfect 42/42 in 1995
Many Fields Medalists are IMO alumni, but high IMO scores don't always predict research success, and vice versa
